narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40—which stands out above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on past students' data to predict bar exam success on the first try. The model assessed your profile, including your lower Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 1st (well below the averages around 5th or 6th), middle family income bracket, notably low LSAT score of 20 (compared to the usual 37), and plan to attend full-time like most applicants. It calculated a 41% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, a risk high enough to trigger the rejection of your admission to protect the school's outcomes. Among all the factors in your background, three stood out as having the strongest sway on this decision. Your undergraduate GPA had the biggest positive impact, strongly pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass, which is a real strength in your favor as a prospective student. However, your Year 3 decile ranking pulled forcefully in the opposite direction, significantly increasing the model's concern about bar failure due to that early underperformance relative to peers. Your middle family income provided a modest counterbalance, nudging things slightly toward success, though not enough to offset the concerns. Overall, these elements combined to tip the scales against you, even with your full-time commitment aligning with the norm for 93% of students. While disheartening, this isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income background—it's the model highlighting patterns from similar profiles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to average, as it weighs heavily in these predictions, and seek ways to improve your academic deciles through targeted prep or additional coursework. You might also explore programs with holistic reviews or appeal with evidence of recent growth. Keep pushing; many overcome early hurdles to excel in law.
